Rule of Thumb: Your result can have no more significance than the least significant number used in the calculation.

Example: The Digital Dilemma
A 12-digit calculator might show \(18.392857^\circ\text{C}\), but if your sensor only has 3 significant figures, the answer is \(18.4^\circ\text{C}\).






Important


Design values (e.g., “a \(1.1\text{k}\Omega\) resistor”) are often treated as exact in theory, but measurements of those components must respect physical limitations.




Students often fall into the trap of providing 10 decimal places because their calculator provides them. It’s vital to emphasize that these extra digits are physically meaningless if the original measurement was only accurate to two or three places.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Statistics: Mean and Standard Deviation
Confidence in measurements is improved via statistical analysis of random errors.
Arithmetic Mean (\(\bar{x}\)): \[\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}\]
Standard Deviation (\(\sigma\)): \[\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}\]
\(\sigma\) measures the spread of data. A smaller \(\sigma\) means the sensor is more precise (readings are tightly clustered).

The arithmetic mean provides the “best estimate” of the true value, while the standard deviation provides the “uncertainty” or “precision” of that estimate. In process control, we use these to determine if a process is stable or if a sensor is failing.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Interactive: The Normal Distribution
Visualize how \(\sigma\) changes the “Peakedness” of sensor readings.

viewof sigma = Inputs.range([0.5, 3.0], {value: 1.0, step: 0.1, label: "Std Deviation (σ)"});













This “Bell Curve” represents random errors. As you decrease sigma, the curve becomes taller and narrower. This represents a “High Quality” or “Precise” sensor. Remember the 68-95-99.7 rule: 99.7% of all readings fall within \(\pm 3\sigma\).

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Case Study: Control System Success
Problem: Evaluate a potato chip packaging system (Goal: 200g).


Before Control

Mean \(\bar{W}_b = 202\text{g}\)
\(\sigma_b = 11\text{g}\)
\(99\%\) range: \(202 \pm 33\text{g}\)


After Control

Mean \(\bar{W}_a = 199\text{g}\)
\(\sigma_a = 5\text{g}\)
\(99\%\) range: \(199 \pm 15\text{g}\)


Conclusion: The system moved the average closer to the target and cut the spread by 50%.

This practical example shows that ECE isn’t just about the electronics; it’s about the data. Even though the mean only moved by 3 grams, the reduction in standard deviation significantly improves the consistency of the product and reduces waste.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Summary and Key Takeaways

Time Response: Sensors are not instantaneous. First-order systems lag; second-order systems oscillate.
Time Constant (\(\tau\)): Defines the “speed” of first-order sensors; 5\(\tau\) is the standard settling time.
Significance: Never report more digits than your measurement hardware can justify.
Statistics: Use \(\bar{x}\) for the average and \(\sigma\) to quantify the reliability/spread of your process.


Wrap up by reminding students that these mathematical tools allow us to predict how a control loop will behave in the real world, where things don’t change instantly and measurements aren’t always perfect.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Problem 1: First-Order Temperature Sensor


Scenario:
A sensor measures temperature linearly:

Transfer Function: \(33\text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}\)
Time Constant (\(\tau\)): \(1.5\text{ s}\)

The Event:
At \(t=0\), the temperature suddenly changes from \(20^\circ\text{C}\) to \(41^\circ\text{C}\).
Task:

Find the sensor output (voltage) at \(t = 0.75\text{ s}\).
Determine the indicated temperature and the error at that moment.








Strategy



Convert temperatures to initial and final voltages (\(b_i, b_f\)).
Apply the first-order equation: \(b(t) = b_i + (b_f - b_i)[1 - e^{-t/\tau}]\)






This problem from Example 15 in the text demonstrates the “lag” error. Even though the environment is at 41 degrees, the sensor is still transitioning. ECE students must understand that the voltage read at t=0.75s is NOT the true process value.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Interactive Solution: Problem 1
Adjust the time \(t\) to see how the voltage and error evolve.

viewof t_query = Inputs.range([0, 7.5], {value: 0.75, step: 0.05, label: "Time t (s)"});













The student can see that at 0.75s (half of one time constant), the error is significant (-12.7 degrees). Note that after 5 time constants (7.5s), the error drops near zero.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Problem 2: Measurement Uncertainty
Scenario:
A digital multimeter (DMM) measures current through a \(12.5\text{-k}\Omega\) resistor as \(2.21\text{ mA}\).

Scale: \(10\text{-mA}\) FS (Full Scale)
Instrument Accuracy: \(\pm 0.2\%\) FS

Task:
Calculate the voltage across the resistor and determine the total uncertainty.






Recall


Accuracy is applied to the Full Scale of the range used, not the reading itself.
\(Uncertainty = 0.002 \times 10\text{ mA} = 0.02\text{ mA}\)




This highlights the difference between reading precision and instrument accuracy. The DMM shows three digits (2.21), but the accuracy of the instrument provides a window of uncertainty around those digits.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Solution: Problem 2


1. Nominal Voltage Calculation:
\(V = I \times R\)
\(V = 2.21\text{ mA} \times 12.5\text{ k}\Omega = 27.625\text{ V}\)
2. Applying Sig Figs:
Since \(2.21\) has 3 sig figs, we round to:
\(V = 27.6\text{ V}\)

3. Uncertainty Calculation:
\(\Delta I = \pm 0.02\text{ mA}\)
Range: \(2.19\text{ mA}\) to \(2.23\text{ mA}\)
\(V_{min} = 2.19 \times 12.5 = 27.375\text{ V}\)
\(V_{max} = 2.23 \times 12.5 = 27.875\text{ V}\)
Result:
\(27.6 \pm 0.3\text{ V}\)


Note how we express the uncertainty (\(\pm 0.3\)) to match the decimal place of our significant figure (\(27.6\)). Reporting \(27.625 \pm 0.25\) would be incorrect engineering practice because it implies more significance than the current measurement supports.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Problem 3: Standard Deviation Analysis
Scenario:
Temperature was measured at 8 locations in a server room to check for “hot spots.”
Data (\(^\circ\text{C}\)): 21.2, 25.0, 18.5, 22.1, 19.7, 27.1, 19.0, 20.0
Task:
Calculate the Arithmetic Mean (\(\bar{x}\)) and the Standard Deviation (\(\sigma\)).






Formula Reminder


\[\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} \quad , \quad \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}\]




In ECE, we often average multiple sensor readings (like in a server room or a chemical reactor) to get a more reliable “global” variable for the control system.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Interactive Solution: Problem 3
Run the code below to calculate the statistics for the room measurements.







The standard deviation of 3.04 degrees tells us how much the temperature varies across the room. If this were a high-precision manufacturing environment, a 3-degree deviation might be unacceptably high, even if the mean (21.6) is on target.

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Problem 4: Evaluating Control Performance
Scenario:
A potato chip bagger (Target = 200g).
Before Control: \(\bar{W} = 202\text{g}, \sigma = 11\text{g}\)
After Control: \(\bar{W} = 199\text{g}, \sigma = 5\text{g}\)
Question:
Using the \(99.7\%\) (\(3\sigma\)) rule, what are the weight ranges before and after?

title: Sensor Time Response and Statistical Analysis
text:
Solution: Problem 4
Solution:

Before: \(202 \pm (3 \times 11) = 202 \pm 33\text{g}\) Range: 169g to 235g
After: \(199 \pm (3 \times 5) = 199 \pm 15\text{g}\) Range: 184g to 214g

Verdict: The system is much more consistent, reducing “underweight” complaints and “overweight” waste.

This concluding problem connects the math to business value. By reducing \(\sigma\) from 11 to 5, the engineer has significantly tightened the production tolerance, which is the primary goal of most process control systems.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Instructional Objectives
The purpose of this presentation is to introduce analog signal-conditioning methods used in process-control systems.

Define the principles of analog signal conditioning.
Analyze the impact of loading on measurement accuracy.
Evaluate passive circuits (dividers, bridges, and filters).
Design simple RC filters for noise reduction.


Welcome to the lecture on Analog Signal Conditioning.
In process control, sensors rarely provide a signal that can be plugged directly into a controller or computer.
Today, we will focus on how we transform those raw sensor signals using passive components like resistors and capacitors to make them usable.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
What is Signal Conditioning?
Signal conditioning refers to operations performed on signals to convert them into a form suitable for interfacing with other elements in a process-control loop.

Conditioned Output: Remains an analog representation of the variable.
Interface Requirement: Crucial before Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC).
Transfer Function: The mathematical relationship between input and output.

Example: A voltage amplifier has a transfer function \(V_{out} = G \cdot V_{in}\).



Even in a digital world, the “front end” is almost always analog.
We use the term ‘Transfer Function’ to describe how the circuit alters the signal.
If we have a sensor outputting 0.2V and we need 5V, our conditioning circuit must have a specific gain and bias.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Common Operations


1. Level & Bias Changes
Adjusting the magnitude (gain) and zero-value (offset). Example: from 0.2 - 0.6V to 0 - 5V

Amplification: Gain \(> 1\)
Attenuation: Gain \(< 1\)

2. Linearization
Converting nonlinear sensor outputs (like CdS cells) into linear representations via circuits or software.

3. Conversions

Resistance to Voltage: Bridge circuits.
Voltage to Current: 4-20 mA standards for transmission.
Voltage to Voltage: from 20-80 mV in sensor to 0-5V in ADC ### 4. Filtering & Matching
Filtering: Removing 60 Hz line noise.
Impedance Matching: Preventing signal loss.



These four pillars define the engineer’s toolkit.
Level and bias adjustments are like the ‘zero’ and ‘span’ on an instrument.
Conversions are vital because while many sensors change resistance, most controllers expect voltage or current.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
The Concept of Loading
Loading occurs when connecting a measurement circuit to a sensor causes the sensor’s output voltage to drop.

FIGURE 2 The Thévenin equivalent circuit of loading.Thévenin’s Theorem Model:
A sensor can be modeled as a voltage source (\(V_x\)) in series with an internal resistance (\(R_x\)).
\[V_y = V_x \left(1 - \frac{R_x}{R_L + R_x}\right)\]
To minimize loading errors, we require \(R_L \gg R_x\).

This is a critical concept for ECE students.
If you use a multimeter with low input impedance to measure a high-impedance source, your reading will be wrong.
Equation 1 shows that if the load resistance RL is not significantly larger than the source resistance Rx, the measured voltage Vy will be lower than the actual source voltage Vx.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Interactive Loading Simulation
Adjust the Load Resistance (\(R_L\)) to see how it affects the measured voltage.
Sensor: \(V_x = 10V\), \(R_{source} = 5k\Omega\).

viewof rl = Inputs.range([100, 100000], {value: 5000, step: 100, label: "Load Resistance (Ω)"});













In this simulation, watch how the error increases as RL approaches 5000 ohms.
When RL equals Rx, the voltage is halved (5V).
This demonstrates why high input impedance is a primary requirement for instrumentation amplifiers.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
3.1 Voltage Divider Circuits



Figure 4: Simple voltage divider used to convert resistance change into voltage change.



Two resistors, \(R_1\) and \(R_2\), in series across supply \(V_s\).
Output is taken across \(R_2\).

Divider equation:
\[
V_D = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_s
\tag{2}
\]
Often, either \(R_1\) or \(R_2\) is the sensor whose resistance varies with the measured variable.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Wheatstone Bridge
Used for high-precision measurement of small resistance changes.

FIGURE 5 The basic dc Wheatstone bridge.Null Condition (\(V_a = V_b\)): \[R_3 R_2 = R_1 R_4\]

The Wheatstone bridge is the gold standard for resistive sensors like strain gauges. Its greatest advantage is that it provides a differential output that is zero when the bridge is balanced. This allows us to amplify only the change in signal.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Bridge Offset Voltage
When the bridge is not balanced, the offset voltage \(\Delta V\) (assuming infinite detector impedance) is:
\[\Delta V = V \left[ \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} - \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4} \right]\]

For small changes (\(\Delta R\)), the output is approximately linear.
For large changes, the output becomes significantly nonlinear.


Note the formula. If all resistors are equal, Delta V is zero. As R4 changes slightly, Delta V changes.
Engineers must decide if the sensor’s range stays within the ‘approximately linear’ region or if software linearization is needed.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Interactive Wheatstone Bridge
Balance the bridge by adjusting \(R_3\).
Given: \(V = 10V\), \(R_1=100\Omega\), \(R_2=100\Omega\), \(R_4 (Sensor)=120\Omega\).

viewof r3_val = Inputs.range([50, 150], {value: 100, step: 0.1, label: "R3 (Ω)"});













Try to find the null point. You’ll notice that as R3 increases, the offset crosses zero.
This balance point is independent of the supply voltage stability, which is why bridges are so stable.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Lead Compensation
In industrial settings, sensors are often far from the bridge circuit. Long wires add resistance that changes with temperature.
Solution: Three-wire connection

Wire 1: In series with \(R_3\).
Wire 2: In series with \(R_4\) (sensor).
Wire 3: Power lead (carries no bridge current).

Since Wire 1 and Wire 2 are identical and experience the same environment, their resistance changes cancel out in the null equation.

This is a classic engineering solution to a “real-world” problem.
By placing one lead in each arm of the bridge, the error becomes a “common-mode” signal that the bridge naturally rejects.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Low-Pass and High-Pass Basics
Used to eliminate unwanted noise (like 60 Hz hum or high-frequency transients).
Critical Frequency (\(f_c\)):
The frequency where output power drops by half (\(-3\text{ dB}\)) or voltage drops to \(0.707 V_{in}\).
\[f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\]

Low-Pass: Passes \(f < f_c\), blocks \(f > f_c\).
High-Pass: Passes \(f > f_c\), blocks \(f < f_c\).


Filters are essential for cleaning up signals.
A low-pass filter is like a sponge that soaks up fast jitters while letting the slow, steady signal through.
We choose R and C values to set the “cutoff” point where this soaking starts.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Filter Response Interactivity
Visualize the frequency response of an RC filter. Adjust \(R\) and \(C\) to move the cutoff.

viewof r_filt = Inputs.range([100, 10000], {value: 1000, step: 100, label: "Resistance (Ω)"});
viewof c_filt = Inputs.range([0.01, 10], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "Capacitance (μF)"});






















The plot shows a Log-Log scale.
Notice that at low frequencies, the gain is 1.
As we pass the critical frequency (fc), the gain drops off at 20dB per decade.
If we need a sharper drop, we would cascade multiple filters.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Advanced Passive Filtering
Cascaded Filters

Used to increase the “sharpness” of the cutoff.
Loading Warning: The second stage loads the first stage.
Resulting gain is the product of individual gains (if isolated).

Band-Pass Filters

Combination of a High-Pass and a Low-Pass in series.
Defined by a lower cutoff \(f_L\) and higher cutoff \(f_H\).

Notch Filters (Twin-T)

Specifically designed to reject a very narrow range of frequencies (e.g., exactly 60 Hz).


A Twin-T notch filter is particularly useful in medical electronics like ECGs, where the 60Hz power line noise is often much larger than the heart signal itself.
However, passive notch filters are sensitive to component tolerances, often requiring precision resistors.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Summary & Key Takeaways

Signal Conditioning is the bridge between the raw sensor and the data acquisition system.
Loading is the enemy of accuracy; always ensure \(R_{in} \gg R_{source}\).
Bridges convert resistance to voltage with high precision and provide a differential “zero” reference.
RC Filters are simple but effective tools for noise management, defined by \(f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)\).


To conclude: always look at the impedances before you connect two circuits.
In our next session, we will look at how Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps) can make these conditioning tasks much easier by providing high input impedance and active filtering.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Problem 1: Loading Effects
Scenario:
A temperature sensor has an output resistance of \(5.0 \, \text{k}\Omega\) and a transfer function of \(20 \, \text{mV/}^\circ\text{C}\).
It is connected to an amplifier with an input resistance of \(10 \, \text{k}\Omega\) and a gain of 10.
Task:
Calculate the actual amplifier output voltage when the temperature is \(50^\circ\text{C}\).

Students often calculate the “ideal” value first (1.0V). The key is to realize the sensor acts as a Thévenin source, and the amplifier’s input resistance acts as a load. This demonstrates that \(R_{in}\) must be much larger than \(R_{source}\) to maintain accuracy.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Solution 1: Loading Effects


Step 1: Ideal Sensor Output
\(V_T = (20 \, \text{mV/}^\circ\text{C}) \times 50^\circ\text{C} = 1.0 \, \text{V}\)
Step 2: Loaded Input (\(V_{in}\))
Using the loading formula:
\(V_{in} = V_T \left( \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_x} \right)\)
\(V_{in} = 1.0 \left( \frac{10 \, \text{k}\Omega}{10 \, \text{k}\Omega + 5 \, \text{k}\Omega} \right) = 0.67 \, \text{V}\)

Step 3: Amplifier Output
\(V_{out} = \text{Gain} \times V_{in}\)
\(V_{out} = 10 \times 0.67 \, \text{V} = 6.7 \, \text{V}\)
Conclusion:
Without considering loading, we would have expected \(10 \, \text{V}\).
The error is a staggering 33%.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Problem 2: Voltage Divider Design
Scenario:
A supply voltage \(V_s = 5.0 \, \text{V}\) is connected to a divider where \(R_1 = 10.0 \, \text{k}\Omega\).
\(R_2\) is a sensor that varies from \(4.0 \, \text{k}\Omega\) to \(12.0 \, \text{k}\Omega\).
Task:

Find the range of the output voltage \(V_D\).
Determine the maximum power dissipated by the sensor.


Dividers are the simplest conversion circuits. Note how the output voltage doesn’t change linearly with R2. Power dissipation is critical to avoid “self-heating” errors in thermal sensors.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Solution 2: Voltage Divider
1. Output Voltage Range:

At \(R_2 = 4 \, \text{k}\Omega\): \(V_{D} = \frac{5 \times 4}{10 + 4} = 1.43 \, \text{V}\)
At \(R_2 = 12 \, \text{k}\Omega\): \(V_{D} = \frac{5 \times 12}{10 + 12} = 2.73 \, \text{V}\)

2. Power Dissipation (\(P = V^2 / R\)):

\(P_{min} = \frac{(1.43)^2}{4000} \approx 0.51 \, \text{mW}\)
\(P_{max} = \frac{(2.73)^2}{12000} \approx 0.62 \, \text{mW}\)







Tip


In ECE design, always check if \(0.62 \, \text{mW}\) exceeds the sensor’s power rating to prevent damage or bias.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Problem 3: Bridge Resolution
Scenario:
A Wheatstone bridge has \(R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 120 \, \Omega\) and \(V = 10.0 \, \text{V}\).
A Digital Voltmeter (DVM) with \(100 \, \mu\text{V}\) resolution is used as the detector.
Task:
Find the smallest measurable change in resistance (\(\Delta R_4\)) that the DVM can detect.

Resolution defines the sensitivity of our measurement system. Even if a sensor changes resistance, if the bridge output change is smaller than the DVM resolution, we can’t see it.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Solution 3: Bridge Resolution
The offset voltage is:
\(\Delta V = \frac{V R_3}{R_1 + R_3} - \frac{V (R_4 - \Delta R)}{R_2 + (R_4 - \Delta R)}\)
Given \(\Delta V = 100 \, \mu\text{V}\), \(V = 10 \, \text{V}\), and \(R = 120 \, \Omega\):
\(10^{-4} = \frac{10(120)}{240} - \frac{10(120 - \Delta R)}{120 + (120 - \Delta R)}\)
\(10^{-4} = 5 - \frac{1200 - 10\Delta R}{240 - \Delta R}\)
Solving for \(\Delta R\):
\(\Delta R \approx 0.0048 \, \Omega\)
Result: The bridge can resolve a resistance change as small as \(4.8 \, \text{m}\Omega\).

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Problem 4: AC Bridge Balance
Scenario:
An AC bridge is at null.
\(Z_1 = 1 \, \text{k}\Omega\) (Resistor). \(Z_2 = 2 \, \text{k}\Omega\) (Resistor). \(Z_3 = 1 \, \text{k}\Omega\) resistor in series with a \(1 \, \mu\text{F}\) capacitor.
Task:
Find the equivalent resistance (\(R_x\)) and capacitance (\(C_x\)) for the unknown arm \(Z_x\).

In AC bridges, we must balance both the real (magnitude) and imaginary (phase) parts. The frequency (\(\omega\)) usually cancels out in simple RC bridges, making the balance independent of frequency.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Solution 4: AC Bridge Balance
Null Condition: \(Z_1 Z_x = Z_2 Z_3\)
\(R_1 (R_x - \frac{j}{\omega C_x}) = R_2 (R_3 - \frac{j}{\omega C})\)
Equating Real Parts:
\(R_1 R_x = R_2 R_3 \implies R_x = \frac{R_2 R_3}{R_1} = \frac{(2\text{k})(1\text{k})}{1\text{k}} = 2 \, \text{k}\Omega\)
Equating Imaginary Parts:
\(\frac{R_1}{\omega C_x} = \frac{R_2}{\omega C} \implies C_x = C \frac{R_1}{R_2} = (1 \, \mu\text{F}) \frac{1\text{k}}{2\text{k}} = 0.5 \, \mu\text{F}\)

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Problem 5: Low-Pass Filter Design
Requirement:
A signal contains data at \(f < 1 \, \text{kHz}\) but has noise at \(1 \, \text{MHz}\). Design a filter that attenuates the \(1 \, \text{MHz}\) noise to \(1\%\) of its original amplitude (\(V_{out}/V_{in} = 0.01\)).
Task:

Calculate the required critical frequency \(f_c\).
Select \(R\) and \(C\) values.


Engineers often start with a desired attenuation at a specific noise frequency. Then they work backward to find the cutoff frequency.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Solution 5: Low-Pass Filter
Step 1: Find \(f_c\)
\(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}\) \(0.01 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (10^6/f_c)^2}} \implies (10^6/f_c)^2 \approx 10,000\)
\(f_c = 10 \, \text{kHz}\)
Step 2: Component Selection
Pick \(C = 0.01 \, \mu\text{F}\):
\(R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (10^4)(10^{-8})} = 1591 \, \Omega\)
Standard Choice: Use \(R = 1.5 \, \text{k}\Omega\) or a \(1.6 \, \text{k}\Omega\) resistor.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Problem 6: Twin-T Notch Filter
Scenario:
An EKG monitor is picking up \(60 \, \text{Hz}\) interference from power lines.
Design a Twin-T notch filter to reject this specific frequency.
Task:

Find \(f_c\) for the Twin-T circuit.
If \(C = 0.1 \, \mu\text{F}\), find \(R, R_1,\) and \(C_1\).


The Twin-T is a special passive filter. Note that the notch frequency \(f_n\) is NOT equal to \(f_c\). It is \(0.785 \times f_c\). This is a common point of confusion for students.

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Solution 6: Twin-T Notch Filter
Step 1: Calculate \(f_c\)
\(f_n = 0.785 f_c \implies 60 = 0.785 f_c\)
\(f_c = 76.43 \, \text{Hz}\)
Step 2: Calculate Components

\(R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (76.43)(10^{-7})} = 20,823 \, \Omega\)
\(R_1 = \frac{\pi R}{10} = \frac{\pi (20823)}{10} \approx 6,541 \, \Omega\)
\(C_1 = \frac{10 C}{\pi} = \frac{10 (0.1 \, \mu\text{F})}{\pi} \approx 0.318 \, \mu\text{F}\)

title: Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Design your own Bridge
Use the editor below to calculate the bridge offset for your own sensor parameters.
Change r_sensor to see the effect on delta_v.







Encourage students to test “unbalanced” bridges. What happens if R1 is doubled? What happens if the supply voltage drops to 5V? This helps them build intuition for sensitivity and supply dependence.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, you should be able to:

Describe the ideal op amp model and the two “golden rules.”
Analyze inverting and noninverting amplifiers and design their resistor values for a desired gain.
Explain non‑ideal op amp parameters (offsets, slew rate, finite gain) and when they matter.
Use op amp building blocks to implement:

Voltage follower
Summing amplifier
Differential and instrumentation amplifiers
Voltage–current and current–voltage converters
Integrators and differentiators

Apply a structured design process to build signal‑conditioning circuits for sensors and bridges.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Why Op Amps in Instrumentation?

Process control and measurement systems need:

Amplification and attenuation
Impedance matching
Level shifting (offsets)
Filtering and linearization
Voltage ↔︎ current conversion

Historically: built from tubes, then discrete transistors
Today: integrated‑circuit (IC) implementations dominate

Small size
Low power
Low cost
Consistent and well‑documented behavior








Tip


Think of an op amp as a universal analog building block you configure with a few external resistors and capacitors to implement many different transfer functions.




Relate this to typical ECE contexts: sensor interfaces, DAQ boards, motor controllers, biomedical instrumentation. Stress that almost all “analog front ends” hide one or more op amps inside.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: 4.1 Op Amp Characteristics – The Black‑Box View
text:
4.1 Op Amp Characteristics – The Black‑Box View


Symbol



Op amp symbol



Two inputs:

+ noninverting
− inverting

One output
Power pins (\(+V_s\), \(-V_s\)) typically not drawn on signal schematics


Transfer Behavior



Op amp transfer



Output vs. differential input \(V_d = V_2 - V_1\)
Large voltage gain in linear region
Saturates at \(\pm V_{\text{sat}}\) set by supply rails



Clarify the mapping between figure labels and our notation: - In the text, \(V_1\) is on the noninverting terminal (+), \(V_2\) is on the inverting terminal (−). - The differential input \(V_d = V_2 - V_1\). - When \(V_2 > V_1\) (more positive at the inverting input), the output goes negative (−). This inversion behavior is the reason for the input naming.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Ideal Op Amp – Key Properties

Infinite open‑loop gain \(A\)

In linear region \(V_{\text{out}} = A \,(V_+ - V_-)\)
Practically \(A \gg 10^5\) even at low frequency

Infinite input impedance

No current flows into \(V_+\) or \(V_-\)

Zero output impedance

Output acts as an ideal voltage source

Infinite bandwidth, zero noise, no offsets (in the ideal model)

These properties lead to two powerful analysis rules when negative feedback is used.






Important


Golden Rules for Ideal Op Amps (with negative feedback):

No current flows into the input terminals: \(I_+ = I_- = 0\).
The inputs are at the same voltage: \(V_+ = V_-\).





Emphasize that these rules are not fundamental device physics; they are approximations that hold because the open‑loop gain is huge and feedback forces the input difference to be near zero. Also highlight: these rules apply only when the op amp is not saturated and there is a feedback path from output to at least one input.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Visualizing Op Amp Saturation












If the differential input is more than a few hundred µV, the output hits \(\pm V_{\text{sat}}\).
Only a very narrow band around \(V_+ \approx V_-\) is linear.


Connect this to comparator behavior: - An op amp with no feedback effectively acts like a comparator: output is either high or low depending on the sign of \(V_+ - V_-\). - For analog amplification applications we always add feedback to force the op amp to operate in the narrow linear region.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Ideal Inverting Amplifier Circuit



Inverting amplifier



Input \(V_{\text{in}}\) goes through \(R_1\) to the summing node.
Feedback resistor \(R_2\) from output back to inverting input.
Noninverting input grounded: \(V_{+} = 0\).

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Ideal Inverting Amplifier – Derivation
At the summing node (inverting input), apply KCL and the two golden rules.

Rule 1: No current into input → sum of resistor currents is zero.
Rule 2: \(V_- = V_+ = 0\) V (since \(V_+ = 0\)).

Currents:

Through \(R_1\): \(I_1 = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}} - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{R_1}\)
Through \(R_2\): \(I_2 = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - 0}{R_2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{R_2}\)

KCL:
\[
I_1 + I_2 = 0 \tag{29}
\]
Substitute:
\[
\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{R_2} = 0 \tag{30}
\]
Solve for \(V_{\text{out}}\):
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{\text{in}} \tag{31}
\]

Gain magnitude: \(\left|\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right| = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\)
180° phase inversion (sign change)







Note


The input impedance seen by \(V_{\text{in}}\) is approximately \(R_1\).




Relate this to a simple ECE example: use an inverting amplifier to scale a 0–5 V DAC output to a 0–10 V or −10 to +10 V range. Also point out that if \(R_2 < R_1\), the circuit attenuates; if \(R_2 > R_1\), it amplifies.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Interactive: Inverting Amplifier Gain Explorer

viewof R1_k = Inputs.range([1, 100], {step: 1, value: 10, label: "R1 (kΩ)"})
viewof R2_k = Inputs.range([1, 100], {step: 1, value: 47, label: "R2 (kΩ)"})






















Have students adjust the sliders and observe how the gain changes with the resistor ratio. Ask: If \(R_1 = 10\) kΩ and \(R_2 = 47\) kΩ, what’s the gain? What happens if we swap them? Reinforce that only the ratio matters for the gain.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: Nonideal Effects – Real Op Amp Behavior
text:
Nonideal Effects – Real Op Amp Behavior


Nonideal I/O characteristic



Nonideal I/O



Finite slope → finite open‑loop gain \(A\)
\(V_{\text{out}}\) saturates at \(\pm V_{\text{sat}}\)
Input offset voltage \(V_{\text{ios}}\) shifts the curve


Nonideal inverting amplifier model



Nonideal inverting amp



Finite input impedance \(z_{\text{in}}\)
Nonzero output impedance \(z_0\)
Finite gain \(A\)


Key definitions:

Open‑loop gain: \[
A = \left|\frac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta(V_2 - V_1)}\right|
    \approx \left|\frac{2 V_{\text{sat}}}{\Delta V}\right|
\]
Typical values:

\(V_{\text{sat}} \approx 10\) V
\(\Delta V \approx 100\,\mu\)V → \(A \approx 200{,}000\)



Explain that closed‑loop gain (with feedback) is mostly set by external resistors; the huge open‑loop gain makes the ideal rules very accurate. Mention that \(z_{\text{in}}\) is often in the MΩ range and \(z_0\) in tens of Ω, so loading effects are small in typical instrumentation applications.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: Nonideal Inverting Amplifier – Impact on Gain
text:
Nonideal Inverting Amplifier – Impact on Gain
Using the nonideal model (Figure 27b), we can derive:
\[
V_0 = -\frac{R_2}{R_1}\left(\frac{1}{1 - \mu}\right) V_{\text{in}} \tag{32}
\]
where
\[
\mu = \frac{\left(1 + \frac{z_0}{R_2}\right)\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1} + \frac{R_2}{z_{\text{in}}}\right)}{A + \frac{z_0}{R_2}} \tag{33}
\]
If \(\mu \ll 1\), the gain reduces to the ideal form:
\[
V_0 \approx -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{\text{in}} \tag{31 revisited}
\]
Example parameter values:

\(A = 200{,}000\)
\(z_0 = 75\,\Omega\)
\(z_{\text{in}} = 2\,\text{M}\Omega\)
Gain \(R_2/R_1 = 100\), with \(R_2 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega\)

Then

\(\mu \approx 0.0005\)
Error in gain ≈ \(0.05\%\)







Tip


For most low‑frequency instrumentation designs, ideal op amp analysis is accurate enough, and we ignore \(\mu\) unless we push bandwidth or precision limits.




If time allows, walk through substituting the numerical values into (33) to show the smallness of \(\mu\). Connect this to why datasheets often say “closed‑loop gain accuracy ±0.1%” for typical resistor tolerances.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
4.2 Practical Op Amp Specifications
Important datasheet parameters beyond gain and impedances:

Input offset voltage \(V_{\text{ios}}\)

Voltage that must be applied between inputs to drive \(V_{\text{out}}\) to zero.

Input offset current

Difference between the two input bias currents that must flow to get \(V_{\text{out}} = 0\).

Input bias current

Average of the two input currents at zero output.

Slew rate (V/µs)

Maximum rate of change of the output voltage for a step input.

Unity‑gain frequency (gain‑bandwidth product)

Frequency where open‑loop gain drops to 1 (0 dB).








Warning


Offset voltages/currents matter most in:

Low‑level signals (mV, µV)
High‑gain DC amplifiers
Precision instrumentation (e.g., strain gauges, biomedical signals)





Use a quick numerical example: - If \(V_{\text{ios}} = 1\) mV and closed‑loop gain is 1000, the output offset can be ~1 V. Motivate the need for offset trimming and careful resistor selection.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: Practical Circuit: Op Amp with Power and Offset Trim
text:
Practical Circuit: Op Amp with Power and Offset Trim



Inverting amp with power and offset trim


Key practical details:

Bipolar supply: \(+V_s\) and \(-V_s\) (often ±12 V, ±15 V, etc.)
Optional offset trim pins to null input offset voltage using a trimmer resistor
Resistor at the noninverting input chosen to match effective resistance at inverting input to partially cancel input bias current errors


Explain how to do the offset‑null procedure:

Set \(V_{\text{in}} = 0\).
Measure \(V_{\text{out}}\).
Adjust the trim pot until \(V_{\text{out}} = 0\).

Once set, the offset is compensated unless the op amp is replaced or temperature changes drastically. Also mention that many modern zero‑drift op amps internally auto‑correct offset, reducing need for external trim.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: “Think mA and kΩ” – Output Current Limits
text:
“Think mA and kΩ” – Output Current Limits
Op amps have limited output current, typically ≈ 20 mA:

Total output current = load current + feedback current
Using very small resistors can demand too much current, causing:

Output voltage droop
Distortion
Potential device damage


Example 17 – Bad vs. Good Design
Desired: inverting amplifier with gain −4.5.

Design equation: \(-4.5 = -R_2/R_1\)

Impractical choice: \(R_1 = 1\,\Omega\), \(R_2 = 4.5\,\Omega\)

For \(V_{\text{in}} = 2\) V: \(V_{\text{out}} = -9\) V
\(I_2 = -9/4.5 = -2\) A (!) → far beyond op amp capability

Practical choice:

\(R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega\), \(R_2 = 4.5\,\text{k}\Omega\)
Same gain, but feedback current is only 2 mA







Important


Rule of thumb: For general‑purpose op amps, choose resistor values such that currents are in the milliamp range and resistances in the kΩ range.




Ask students: If we used megaohm‑range resistors instead, what new problem might we encounter? (Answer: more thermal noise, higher sensitivity to bias currents and leakage.) So there is a tradeoff: avoid both extremely low and extremely high resistor values.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: 5. Op Amp Circuits in Instrumentation – Overview
text:
5. Op Amp Circuits in Instrumentation – Overview
Common building blocks:

Voltage follower (buffer) – unity gain, high input Z, low output Z.
Inverting amplifier – gain set by ratio \(R_2/R_1\), can attenuate or amplify.
Summing amplifier – weighted sum of multiple inputs.
Noninverting amplifier – high input Z, gain ≥ 1.
Differential and instrumentation amplifiers – measure differences, reject common mode.
Voltage‑to‑current (V–I) and current‑to‑voltage (I–V) converters – common in 4–20 mA loops.
Integrator and differentiator – time‑domain operations.
Nonlinear / linearization circuits – e.g., logarithmic amplifiers.

We will walk through each quickly with formulas and applications.

Emphasize that you don’t need to memorize every configuration; you should recognize patterns and know where to look up exact formulas. Also, instrumentation often chains these blocks: e.g., sensor → bridge → instrumentation amp → filter → V–I converter.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Voltage Follower (Buffer)



Voltage follower



Output tied directly to inverting input.
Input applied to noninverting input.

Using rules: \(V_{+} = V_{-} = V_{\text{in}}\), and feedback enforces \(V_{\text{out}} = V_{-}\).
So: \[
V_{\text{out}} \approx V_{\text{in}}
\]

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
5.2 Inverting Amplifier & Summing Amplifier
Recall:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{\text{in}} \tag{31}
\]
Summing Amplifier (two inputs example, Figure 31):



Summing amplifier


Transfer function:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\left[ \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_2}{R_3} V_2 \right] \tag{34}
\]
Special cases:

\(R_1 = R_2 = R_3\) → \(V_{\text{out}} = -(V_1 + V_2)\) (inverted sum)
Other resistor choices provide weighted sums or averages







Note


Summing amplifiers are useful for:

Adding a DC offset (bias) to a signal
Mixing multiple sensor signals
Implementing simple DACs (weighted binary inputs)





Tie this to digital‑to‑analog conversion: A R‑2R ladder plus an op amp summing node can form a DAC. Also connect to Example 18 in the text (implementing \(V_{\text{out}} = 3.4V_{\text{in}} + 5\) using a summing amplifier followed by an inverter).

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example: Implementing ( V_{} = 3.4V_{} + 5 )
Strategy (as described in text):

Use a summing amplifier with two inputs: \(V_{\text{in}}\) and a fixed 5 V.

Gains chosen so output after the summing stage is \(-(3.4V_{\text{in}} + 5)\).

Follow with an inverting amplifier of gain −1 to remove the sign.




Circuit for Example 18


Resistor selection uses the same “mA and kΩ” philosophy.

Ask students to sketch numerical resistor values: - Suppose we choose \(R_{\text{f}} = 10\) kΩ. - For input 1 (signal), choose \(R_1\) so that \(R_{\text{f}}/R_1 = 3.4\). - For input 2 (5 V), choose \(R_3\) so that \(R_{\text{f}}/R_3 = 1\). Then add a unity‑gain inverting stage.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
5.3 Noninverting Amplifier



Noninverting amplifier



Input \(V_{\text{in}}\) applied to noninverting (+) input
Feedback and reference resistors \(R_2\) and \(R_1\) form a divider from \(V_{\text{out}}\) to ground
Summing point at inverting input has voltage \(V_S\)

KCL at node S and ideal equality \(V_- = V_+ = V_{\text{in}}\):
\[
\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}}{R_2} = 0
\]
Solving:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \left[1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right] V_{\text{in}} \tag{35}
\]

Gain ≥ 1, cannot attenuate
Input impedance: very high (≈ op amp input Z)
Output impedance: low







Tip


Use noninverting amplifiers when you need high input impedance and positive gain (no phase inversion).




Connect to sensor interfaces: if your sensor is a weak voltage source, you typically buffer it (voltage follower) or amplify it using a noninverting stage. Also mention that to attenuate you must either use a passive divider or an inverting stage with \(R_2 < R_1\).

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example: Noninverting Gain of 42
Design a high‑impedance amplifier with voltage gain 42.
We use:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \left[1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right] V_{\text{in}}
\]
So
\[
42 = 1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1} \Rightarrow R_2 = 41 R_1
\]
Choose \(R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega\), then \(R_2 = 41\,\text{k}\Omega\).

Input impedance ≈ op amp input impedance (>> MΩ).


You can ask students: could we instead choose \(R_1 = 2\) kΩ and \(R_2 = 82\) kΩ? Yes; the gain is still 42. Discuss tradeoffs in resistor sizes (noise, bias current effects).

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
5.4 Differential Amplifier – Core Concept
Goal: Amplify the difference between two voltages while rejecting any common‑mode voltage.
Ideal relationship:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = A (V_a - V_b) \tag{36}
\]
Common‑mode input voltage:
\[
V_{cm} = \frac{V_a + V_b}{2} \tag{37}
\]
Common‑mode rejection ratio (CMRR):
\[
\text{CMRR} = \frac{A}{A_{cm}} \tag{38}
\]
Common‑mode rejection (CMR) in dB:
\[
\text{CMR} = 20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR}) \tag{39}
\]
Typical CMR for instrumentation amplifiers: 60–100 dB.






Important


High CMR is critical when your sensor signal is small but rides on a large common‑mode voltage (e.g., Wheatstone bridge outputs, biomedical electrodes, strain gauges).




Use simple numeric examples given in the text to show that a 0.1 V difference on 7.8 V vs. 7.7 V and −2.4 V vs. −2.5 V all yield the same differential output, while an ideal differential amplifier ignores the large common level.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Differential Amplifier Circuit



Differential amplifier



Requires two pairs of matched resistors: \(R_1\) and \(R_2\) on each side
Ideal transfer function (Appendix 6 derivation):

\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1) \tag{40}
\]
Notes:

Resistor matching quality strongly affects CMR.
Input impedances are not very high and not equal between inputs.
For instrumentation, we often add input buffers to make input impedance high and symmetric.


Discuss practical implementation: often use thin‑film resistor networks with tight ratio tolerances (0.01% or better) to achieve high CMR. Point out that with discrete 1% resistors, the achievable CMR can be limited by mismatch.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Instrumentation Amplifier – Buffered Differential Amp



Instrumentation amplifier with followers



Add voltage followers (buffers) before differential stage:

Greatly increases input impedance (ideal for sensor bridges).
Input loading becomes negligible.

Transfer function remains:

\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1) \tag{40 again}
\]
Disadvantage:

Changing gain requires changing both \(R_1\) resistors and keeping them matched.


Mention that many “3‑op‑amp instrumentation amplifier” ICs are based on this concept, but use a clever input stage to allow gain to be set by a single resistor. That configuration appears in Figure 37 later.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example 20 – Sensor 20–250 mV → 0–5 V
Sensor: \(V_{\text{in}}\) from 20 mV to 250 mV. Desired: \(V_{\text{out}}\) from 0 to 5 V, very high input impedance.
Model: linear relationship
\[
V_{\text{out}} = m V_{\text{in}} + V_0
\]
Use two points:
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 &= m(0.020) + V_0 \\
5 &= m(0.250) + V_0
\end{aligned}
\]
Solve:

\(V_0 = -0.434\) V
\(m = 21.7\)

So:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = 21.7 V_{\text{in}} - 0.434
\]
Alternative form:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = 21.7(V_{\text{in}} - 0.02)
\]
This looks like:

Differential amplifier with gain 21.7
Fixed 0.02 V offset applied to inverting input




Solution circuit Example 20



Point out how a zener diode plus resistor divider is used to create a stable 0.02 V reference, so supply variations don’t distort the mapping. Also highlight that an off‑the‑shelf instrumentation amplifier IC could implement this with fewer discrete parts.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: Instrumentation Amplifier with Single Gain Resistor
text:
Instrumentation Amplifier with Single Gain Resistor



3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier



Two input stages: noninverting amplifiers with shared gain‑setting resistor \(R_G\)
Third stage: differential amplifier with resistors \(R_2\) and \(R_3\)

Transfer function:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1) \tag{41}
\]
Advantages:

Gain adjusted by single resistor \(R_G\)
High input impedance
High CMR, mainly dependent on matching of \(R_2\) and \(R_3\)

Many IC instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, INA128) use this topology.

Explain that IC manufacturers can laser‑trim \(R_2\) and \(R_3\) on‑chip to excellent matching, so the user just chooses \(R_G\) to set the desired gain without sacrificing CMR.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example 21 – Instrumenting a Wheatstone Bridge
Bridge (Figure 38): \(R_4\) varies from 100 Ω to 102 Ω.



Bridge


Bridge voltage: \(V_a - V_b = \Delta V\).
At \(R_4 = 102\,\Omega\) and \(R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 100\,\Omega\), supply = 5 V:
\[
\Delta V = 5\left[\frac{100}{100+100} - \frac{102}{100+102}\right] = -24.75\text{ mV}
\]
Need: \(V_{\text{out}} = 0\) V at 100 Ω, \(V_{\text{out}} = 2.5\) V at 102 Ω.
So differential gain:
\[
A = \frac{2.5\text{ V}}{24.75\text{ mV}} \approx 101
\]
Using Eq. (41) with \(R_2 = R_3 = 1\text{ k}\Omega\), \(R_1 = 100\text{ k}\Omega\):
\[
101 = \left(1 + \frac{2 \cdot 100{,}000}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{1000}{1000}\right)
\]
Solve for \(R_G\):
\[
R_G = 2000\,\Omega
\]
Polarity: connect bridge nodes such that \(V_b\) goes to the input that yields positive output as \(R_4\) increases.

Have students sanity‑check: a 2% change in resistance (100 → 102 Ω) yields only 24.75 mV at the bridge, which must be amplified by ≈ 100× to reach 2.5 V — a classic instrumentation amplifier application.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Voltage‑to‑Current Converter



V–I converter


We want current \(I\) through a load to be a function of an input voltage \(V_{\text{in}}\), roughly independent of the load.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example 22 – 0–1 V to 0–10 mA Converter
Requirements:

Input: 0–1 V signal
Output: 0–10 mA current
Op amp saturation: ±10 V

Strategy:

The V–I converter inverts (negative sign in Eq. 42), so we first invert the 0–1 V to 0 to −1 V with a simple inverting amplifier.
Choose \(R_1 = R_2\) in the V–I converter:
Then Eq. (42) becomes: \[
I = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 R_3}V_{\text{in}} = -\frac{1}{R_3}V_{\text{in}}
\]
For 10 mA at 1 V (actually −1 V at the converter input):
\[
R_3 = \frac{1\text{ V}}{10\text{ mA}} = 100\,\Omega
\]
Choose \(R_5 = 0\) so Eq. (43) simplifies to \(R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_4\). With \(R_1=R_2\), we get \(R_3 = R_4 = 100\,\Omega\).
Maximum load resistance (Eq. 44):
\[
R_{ml} = 100\left[\frac{10\text{ V}}{10\text{ mA}} - 100\right] / 200 = 450\,\Omega
\]

So the loop can drive up to 10 mA into a 450 Ω load.

Ask: what happens if the cable and receiver resistance exceed 450 Ω? The op amp will saturate before delivering full 10 mA, causing a nonlinear transfer and limiting measurement range.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Current‑to‑Voltage Converter


Simple configuration:

Unknown current \(I\) is injected into inverting node through resistor \(R\).
Noninverting input is at ground (or reference).

Using ideal rules:

Node voltage ≈ 0 V (virtual ground).
Current through \(R\) equals input current \(I\).
Output adjusts so that \(I R + V_{\text{out}}/0 = 0\) ⇒ \[
V_{\text{out}} = -IR
\]





I–V converter



Used heavily for receiving 4–20 mA process‑control signals: choose \(R\) so that 4–20 mA maps to the desired voltage (e.g., 1–5 V).

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Integrator



Integrator



Input resistor \(R\), feedback capacitor \(C\).

Node equation:
\[
\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{R} + C\frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt} = 0
\]

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example 23 – 10 V/ms Ramp
Goal: ramp increasing at 10 V/ms.
Desired:
\[
\frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt} = 10\text{ V/ms}
\]
Use integrator:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{RC} t
\]
Let \(RC = 1\) ms and \(V_{\text{in}} = -10\) V. Then:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{-10}{1\text{ ms}} t = 10\frac{\text{V}}{\text{ms}}t
\]
Example component choices:

\(R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega\), \(C = 1\,\mu\text{F}\) → \(RC = 1\) ms


Stress that in practice the ramp cannot grow forever; the op amp saturates at some ±\(V_{\text{sat}}\). A reset (switching \(C\) to discharge) is often used.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Differentiator



Differentiator



Input capacitor \(C\), feedback resistor \(R\).

Node equation:
\[
C\frac{dV_{\text{in}}}{dt} + \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{R} = 0
\]

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
5.9 Op Amp Linearization & Nonlinear Circuits
Idea: place a nonlinear element in the feedback loop of an op amp.



Nonlinear amplifier


Summing point equation:
\[
\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{R} + I(V_{\text{out}}) = 0 \tag{51}
\]
Solve for \(V_{\text{out}}\):
\[
V_{\text{out}} = G\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{R}\right) \tag{52}
\]
Where \(G\) is related to the inverse of \(I(V_{\text{out}})\).
Example: diode in feedback → logarithmic amplifier.



Diode feedback log amp


Diode equation:
\[
I(V_{\text{out}}) = I_0 \exp(\alpha V_{\text{out}}) \tag{53}
\]
Substituting and inverting:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{1}{\alpha}\ln(V_{\text{in}}) - \frac{1}{\alpha}\ln(I_0 R) \tag{54}
\]
→ Output is logarithmic in input.

Mention uses of log amps: - Expanding dynamic range (e.g., audio compression, power meters) - Converting multiplication/division to addition/subtraction in analog computers. Linearization: by appropriately choosing or designing nonlinear feedback, you can make a nonlinear sensor’s output more nearly linear.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: 6. Design Guidelines for Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
6. Design Guidelines for Analog Signal Conditioning



Measurement & S/C model


General workflow:

Define the measurement objective
Select the sensor (if not fixed)
Design the analog S/C block
Check accuracy, loading, noise, and practical constraints

We’ll summarize each step as a design checklist.

Encourage students to explicitly write each of these items when given a design problem; it clarifies assumptions and avoids hidden requirements.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Step 1 – Define the Measurement Objective

Parameter

What are we measuring? Temperature, pressure, flow, position, voltage, current…

Range

Min/max of the physical variable: e.g., 100–200 °C, 45–85 psi.

Accuracy

Required error bounds: e.g., ±5% full scale (FS), ±3% of reading.

Linearity

Must the mapping from parameter to output be linear?

Noise environment

Expected interference (EMI), frequency content, and noise levels.








Tip


Clearly stating these before drawing circuits avoids under‑ or over‑design (e.g., using an ultra‑precision amp when 5% error is acceptable).




Give a quick example: - “Measure water level between 0.5–2.0 m with ±1 cm accuracy; output 1–5 V; environment: industrial plant with motor noise.” From this, we know we need good EMI immunity, likely shielded cables and filtering.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Step 2 – Understand / Select the Sensor
If the sensor is given, document its characteristics; if not, choose an appropriate one.

Output parameter

Does the sensor produce resistance, voltage, current, frequency, etc.?

Transfer function

Relationship between input (physical) and output (electrical).
Linear? Nonlinear? Given by equation or table?

Time response

First‑order time constant, 2nd‑order dynamics, bandwidth.

Range

Range of sensor output over measurement range.

Power limits

Max dissipation, max current/voltage, self‑heating constraints.



Highlight that many sensor datasheets specify maximum excitation current or power; exceeding that can change the reading (self‑heating) or permanently damage the sensor.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: Step 3 – Design the Analog Signal Conditioning
text:
Step 3 – Design the Analog Signal Conditioning

Desired output parameter

Usually a voltage for ADCs (e.g., 0–5 V, ±10 V).
Sometimes current (4–20 mA) or frequency.

Output range

Ensure mapping covers full ADC input range for better resolution.

Input impedance of S/C

Must not excessively load sensor or bridge.

Output impedance of S/C

Must be low enough for the next stage (ADC, recorder, actuator driver).








Note


For voltage‑output sensors, high input impedance is often crucial. For bridge‑based sensors, design excitation and bridge resistances to respect sensor power limits and linearity.




Reinforce the “develop an equation first” approach: Write \(V_{\text{out}} = m (\text{sensor variable}) + V_0\), then implement it with op amp blocks.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
section: Step 4 – Additional Analog S/C Design Notes
text:
Step 4 – Additional Analog S/C Design Notes

If input is a resistance change (e.g., RTD, strain gauge):


Design the bridge/divider carefully:

Check nonlinearity of bridge output vs. resistance.
Verify sensor self‑heating power is within limits.



For the op amp section:


Start with a static transfer function (equation, straight line).
Then choose topologies (inverting, noninverting, summing, differential).


Always verify loading effects:


Does S/C input load the sensor?
Does S/C output properly drive the load/ADC?


Encourage students to always sketch a simple block diagram before going into component‑level detail; then annotate each block with its assumed input/output impedance, gain, and ranges.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example 24 – −2.4 to −1.1 V → 0 to 2.5 V
We only know we need a voltage‑to‑voltage conversion; sensor details are unknown.

Good practice: assume high source impedance → design high input Z using a voltage follower.

Model:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = m V_{\text{in}} + V_0
\]
Using:
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -2.4 m + V_0 \\
2.5 &= -1.1 m + V_0
\end{aligned}
\]
From first: \(V_0 = 2.4m\). Sub into second:
\[
2.5 = -1.1 m + 2.4 m = 1.3 m \Rightarrow m = 1.923
\]
Then \(V_0 = 4.615\) V, so:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = 1.923 V_{\text{in}} + 4.615
\]
One implementation (Figure 46):

Input buffer (voltage follower) → high input Z
Summing amplifier → implements \(1.923 V_{\text{in}} + 4.615\) (but inverted)
Inverting amplifier → flips sign back




Solution Example 24



Note the use of resistor dividers and trimmer potentiometers to create the 4.615 V bias, assuming a 15 V supply. Prompt students to design the alternative approach using a differential amplifier as suggested: \(V_{\text{out}} = 1.923 (V_{\text{in}} + 2.4)\) → instrumentation or differential amplifier with a fixed 2.4 V on one input.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Example 25 – Temperature Measurement System
Given:

Temperature range: 250–450 °C
Accuracy: ±2 °C
Sensor: resistance varies linearly from 280 Ω to 1060 Ω over that range
Max sensor power: 5 mW
Desired output: −5 to +5 V, linear
Load: high‑impedance recorder

Step 1 – Limit Sensor Power
Power:
\[
P = I^2 R
\]
At maximum resistance (1060 Ω), limiting case:
\[
I_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{0.005}{1060}} \approx 2.17\text{ mA}
\]
So design such that sensor current always < 2 mA.
Step 2 – Transfer Function
Relate \(V_{\text{out}}\) to \(R_s\).
Model:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = m R_s + V_0
\]
Two conditions:
\[
\begin{aligned}
-5 &= 280m + V_0 \\
+5 &= 1060m + V_0
\end{aligned}
\]
Subtract:
\[
10 = 780m \Rightarrow m = 0.0128
\]
Then:
\[
V_0 = -5 - 280(0.0128) = -8.58
\]
So:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = 0.0128 R_s - 8.58
\]
Step 3 – Circuit Realization



Solution Example 25



Use an inverting amplifier with sensor \(R_s\) in feedback and a fixed input of 1 V through 1 kΩ → sensor current = 1 mA (< 2 mA).
Follow with a second stage (inverting summer) to achieve the correct offset and sign.
Trimmer resistors used to precisely set 1.00 V and 8.58 V biases.


Emphasize: the first stage essentially converts resistance to voltage linearly (via \(V = I R_s\)), while keeping current safe. The second stage re‑scales and offsets that voltage to the desired −5 to +5 V window. Remind students that there are multiple valid designs (e.g., bridge + instrumentation amplifier). Cost, complexity, and accuracy tradeoffs decide which is best.

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Summary / Key Points

Op amp basics: treat the op amp as a high‑gain differential amplifier; with feedback, we can assume \(V_{+}=V_{-}\) and no input current for ideal analysis.
Core configurations:

Voltage follower: unity gain, very high input Z, low output Z.
Inverting amplifier: gain \(-R_2/R_1\), input Z ≈ \(R_1\).
Noninverting amplifier: gain \(1+R_2/R_1\), very high input Z.
Summing amplifier: weighted sum of inputs.

Differential and instrumentation amplifiers:

Extract small differences (\(V_a - V_b\)) in presence of large common‑mode voltage.
CMR/CMRR key metrics; resistor matching critical.
3‑op‑amp instrumentation amps provide high Z, adjustable gain via \(R_G\).

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Formula Summary
Ideal Op Amp Golden Rules (with negative feedback):

\(I_{+} = I_{-} = 0\)
\(V_{+} = V_{-}\)

Inverting Amplifier:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1}V_{\text{in}}, \quad Z_{\text{in}} \approx R_1
\]
Noninverting Amplifier:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)V_{\text{in}}
\]
Summing Amplifier (2‑input):
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}V_1 + \frac{R_2}{R_3}V_2\right)
\]

title: Operational Amplifiers in Instrumentation
text:
Formula Summary (cont.)
Voltage‑to‑Current Converter
\[
I = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 R_3} V_{\text{in}} \tag{42}
\]
Resistor condition:
\[
R_1 (R_3 + R_5) = R_2 R_4 \tag{43}
\]
Maximum load resistance:
\[
R_{ml} = \frac{(R_4 + R_5)\left[\dfrac{V_{\text{sat}}}{I_m} - R_3\right]}{R_3 + R_4 + R_5} \tag{44}
\]
Current‑to‑Voltage Converter
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -I R \tag{45}
\]
Integrator
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{\text{in}}\,dt \tag{47}
\]
Differentiator
\[
V_{\text{out}} = -RC \frac{dV_{\text{in}}}{dt} \tag{50}
\]
Logarithmic Amplifier (diode feedback)
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{1}{\alpha}\ln(V_{\text{in}}) - \frac{1}{\alpha}\ln(I_0 R) \tag{54}
\]

Encourage students to keep a personal “formula sheet” summarizing these relationships; in practice, engineers repeatedly reuse these few core expressions across many designs.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, you should be able to:

Explain how comparators form the “front end” between analog sensors and digital logic.
Design simple alarm and hysteresis (Schmitt trigger) circuits with comparators.
Compute the output of unipolar and bipolar DACs and select resolution (number of bits).
Compute the digital output of an ADC for a given analog input and reference.
Compare successive‑approximation and dual‑slope ADCs and predict their conversion time.
Analyze how conversion time limits the maximum signal frequency and why sample‑and‑hold is needed.
Describe frequency‑based A/D conversion using counters and V‑to‑F converters.


These objectives map to practical instrumentation tasks: alarm design, driving actuators from a microcontroller, choosing ADC/DAC resolutions, and understanding the constraints of data acquisition hardware. Emphasize that every measurement or actuator they use in embedded systems ultimately passes through these building blocks.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Big Picture: Why Converters?

Sensors usually output analog (voltages, currents).
Microcontrollers, FPGAs, and PLCs speak digital (bits, words).
We need two “translators”:

ADC – Analog to Digital (sensor → computer).
DAC – Digital to Analog (computer → actuator).




Typical process‑control loop

Sensor → analog signal
Signal conditioning (amp, filter)
ADC → digital data
Digital controller (μC / PLC)
DAC → analog command
Actuator (valve, motor drive, heater, etc.)




















Important


Key idea: Every loop that uses a digital controller has at least one ADC and often a DAC.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Roadmap for This Session

Comparators – the simplest analog/digital interface.
DACs – digital words → analog voltages.
ADCs – analog voltages → digital words (unipolar and bipolar).
ADC architectures & timing – successive approximation, dual‑slope.
Sample‑and‑hold – freezing the analog value so ADCs don’t get confused.
Frequency‑based converters – when it’s easier to count pulses than measure volts.


Set expectations: we will not memorize every formula by rote. Instead, I’ll highlight which ones are “core tools” that you’ll re‑use in design, exams, and labs.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
3.1 Comparators – The Simplest Analog/Digital Bridge
A comparator compares two voltages and outputs a digital‑like signal.

Inputs:

\(V_a\) and \(V_b\) – analog voltages.

Output:

Logic‑level high (1) if one input is larger.
Logic‑level low (0) otherwise.





Figure 6: Basic comparator compares voltages and produces a digital output.








Note


Comparators are often used as:

Alarm generators.
Zero‑crossing detectors.
Building blocks inside ADCs and DACs.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Comparator Operation
Let’s define:

\(V_{in}\) – variable sensor voltage.
\(V_{ref}\) – fixed reference / trip voltage.

Typical behavior (non‑inverting input as signal):

If \(V_{in} > V_{ref}\) → output high (≈ logic “1”).
If \(V_{in} < V_{ref}\) → output low (≈ logic “0”).













Mention voltage levels: with a 5 V supply, “high” may be near 5 V, “low” near 0 V, but check datasheet for actual thresholds and output structures.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Example 7: Temperature & Pressure Alarm – Setup
Spec:

Trigger alarm if

Temperature \(T > 160^\circ\mathrm{C}\) and
Pressure \(P > 10\ \mathrm{kPa}\).


Transducers:

Temperature: \(V_T = (2.2\ \mathrm{mV} / {}^\circ\mathrm{C}) \cdot T\).
Pressure: \(V_P = (0.2\ \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{kPa}) \cdot P\).

Goal: Design an alarm system using comparators and logic.






Tip


Think of each comparator as a “threshold detector” for a single variable. Then combine their outputs with logic gates (AND, OR).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: Example 7: Alarm Design – Voltages and Logic
text:
Example 7: Alarm Design – Voltages and Logic

Convert the temperature limit to volts:

\[
V_{T,\text{trip}} = (2.2\ \mathrm{mV/^\circ C})(160^\circ \mathrm{C}) = 0.352\ \mathrm{V}
\]

Convert the pressure limit to volts:

\[
V_{P,\text{trip}} = (0.2\ \mathrm{V/kPa})(10\ \mathrm{kPa}) = 2.0\ \mathrm{V}
\]

Use two comparators:


Temp comparator: outputs 1 when \(V_T > 0.352\ \mathrm{V}\).
Pressure comparator: outputs 1 when \(V_P > 2.0\ \mathrm{V}\).


Use an AND gate to combine both conditions.




Figure 7: Example 7 alarm using two comparators and an AND gate.



Emphasize: this is a digital logic condition applied to analog thresholds. Ask students: How would you modify this if you wanted an alarm on “OR” condition instead?

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Open‑Collector Comparator Outputs





Figure 8a: Comparator with open-collector output.





Figure 8b: Pull-up resistor provides logic levels.




Some comparators expose only the collector of an internal NPN transistor at the output.
Need an external pull‑up resistor \(R_{\text{pull}}\) to a supply \(V_s\).

Behavior:

Transistor ON → output pulled ≈ 0 V → logic 0.
Transistor OFF → output pulled up to \(V_s\) → logic 1.

Advantages:

Can use different logic supply than comparator’s internal supply

e.g., comparator powered at 5 V, but output pulls up to 12 V to drive a relay.

Can wire‑OR several outputs by tying open collectors together with one pull‑up.








Tip


Open‑collector outputs are very common on comparators and some logic ICs used for interrupt lines, wired‑OR flags, and relays/LED drivers.




Discuss how this is effectively a low‑side switch. Stress that without a pull‑up resistor, you will not see any valid logic level at the pin. Also mention you must size the resistor to limit current when the transistor saturates.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Comparator “Jiggle” Problem – Noise Around Threshold
If the input signal is noisy or moves slowly through \(V_{ref}\):

Small noise can repeatedly push \(V_{in}\) above and below \(V_{ref}\).
Output toggles rapidly: “jiggles” or chatter.




Figure 9: Noisy input causing comparator output jitter.


This is bad for:

Alarm relays (chattering contacts).
Interrupt lines to microcontrollers (too many events).
Motor/valve control (rapid on–off cycling).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Hysteresis Comparator (Schmitt Trigger) – Concept
Solution: add hysteresis (a deadband window).

Two thresholds instead of one:

Upper threshold \(V_H\): trips output high.
Lower threshold \(V_L\): trips output low.

Once output changes state, you require a larger change in input to switch it back.




Figure 10a: Hysteresis comparator circuit.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Hysteresis Comparator – Equations
With resistors \(R\) and feedback \(R_f\) (and \(R_f \gg R\)):

Upper threshold (going up):

\[
V_H = V_{\text{ref}}
\]

Lower threshold (coming down):

\[
V_L = V_{\text{ref}} - \frac{R}{R_f} V_0
\]

Hysteresis width (deadband):

\[
\Delta V_{\text{hyst}} = V_H - V_L = \frac{R}{R_f} V_0
\]



Figure 10b: Hysteresis input–output relationship.








Tip


Design procedure:

Choose desired deadband \(\Delta V_{\text{hyst}}\).
Use $ (R/R_f) V_0 = V_{} $ to pick a reasonable \(R_f\), then compute \(R\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: Example: Tank Level with Splashing (Hysteresis Design)
text:
Example: Tank Level with Splashing (Hysteresis Design)
Sensor: \(V = 20\ \mathrm{mV/cm} \cdot \text{level}\).
Specs:

Comparator should go high (5 V) at level \(50\ \mathrm{cm}\).
Level fluctuates by \(\pm 3\ \mathrm{cm}\) due to splashing.


Nominal reference at 50 cm:

\[
V_{\text{ref}} = (20\ \mathrm{mV/cm})(50\ \mathrm{cm}) = 1.0\ \mathrm{V}
\]

Splash noise amplitude:

\[
\pm 3\ \mathrm{cm} \Rightarrow \Delta V_{\text{noise}} = (20\ \mathrm{mV/cm})(\pm 3) = \pm 60\ \mathrm{mV}
\]
Total span = \(120\ \mathrm{mV}\). Choose deadband slightly larger: e.g., \(150\ \mathrm{mV}\).

Design resistors:

\[
(R/R_f)(5\ \mathrm{V}) = 150\ \mathrm{mV} \Rightarrow R/R_f = 0.03
\]
Pick \(R_f = 100\ \mathrm{k\Omega}\) → \(R = 3\ \mathrm{k\Omega}\).
Use these in the circuit of Figure 10a.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Quick Interactive Check – Comparator Threshold







Invite students to edit sensor_scale_mV_per_unit, threshold_unit, and noise_unit and run to see how required hysteresis changes.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
3.2 Digital‑to‑Analog Converters (DACs) – Concept
A DAC converts an n‑bit digital word into a proportional analog voltage.

Input: \(b_1 b_2 \ldots b_n\) (LSB to MSB).
Reference voltage: \(V_R\).
Output: stepwise voltage \(V_{\text{out}}\).

Unipolar DAC (0 to near \(V_R\)):
\[
V_{\text{out}} = V_R \left[b_1 2^{-1} + b_2 2^{-2} + \dots + b_n 2^{-n}\right]
\tag{5}
\]
Alternative form using integer \(N\) (0 to \(2^n-1\)):
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{N}{2^n} V_R
\tag{6}
\]






Note


Think of \(N/2^n\) as the fraction of full scale.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Unipolar DAC Range and Resolution

Minimum output:

\(N = 0\) → \(V_{\text{out}} = 0\).

Maximum output:

\(N = 2^n - 1\) →


\[
V_{\max} = \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}V_R = \left(1 - 2^{-n}\right)V_R
\]
Example:

4‑bit DAC:

\[
V_{\max} = (1 - 2^{-4})V_R = 0.9375 V_R
\]

8‑bit DAC:

\[
V_{\max} \approx 0.9961 V_R
\]
Resolution (step size):
\[
\Delta V_{\text{out}} = V_R 2^{-n}
\tag{8}
\]

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Example: Simple 8‑bit DAC Output
8‑bit, \(V_R = 5.0\ \mathrm{V}\), input word \(10100111_2\).

Compute \(N\):

\[
10100111_2 = 167_{10}
\]

Use Equation (6):

\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{167}{256} \cdot 5.0 \approx 3.2617\ \mathrm{V}
\]

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Example 9: 10‑bit DAC with 10 V Reference
Given: 10‑bit DAC, \(V_R = 10.0\ \mathrm{V}\).

Input = 0B5H \(= 0010110101_2\). Compute \(V_{\text{out}}\) using Equation (5).

\[
V_{\text{out}} = 10.0\left[2^{-3} + 2^{-5} + 2^{-6} + 2^{-8} + 2^{-10}\right]
\]
\[
V_{\text{out}} = 10.0[0.1767578] \approx 1.7676\ \mathrm{V}
\]

Input = 20FH = \(527_{10}\). Use Equation (6):

\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{527}{1024} \cdot 10.0 \approx 5.1465\ \mathrm{V}
\]

What input \(N\) to get 6.5 V?

\[
N = 2^n \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_R} = 1024 \cdot \frac{6.5}{10} = 665.6
\]
We must use integers → cannot get exactly 6.5 V.

\(N = 665\) → \(\approx 6.494\ \mathrm{V}\).
\(N = 666\) → \(\approx 6.504\ \mathrm{V}\).







Warning


Important: Finite resolution → some target voltages are not exactly representable.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Bipolar DAC – Output Around Zero
Sometimes we need output in \(-V_{\text{max}}\) to \(+V_{\text{max}}\).
Typical bipolar DAC uses offset binary:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{N}{2^n} V_R - \frac{1}{2} V_R
\tag{7}
\]

\(N = 0\) → \(V_{\text{out}} = -V_R/2\).
\(N = 2^n - 1\) →

\[
V_{\text{out}}(\max) = \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2^n}\right)V_R
\]
So positive side is slightly less than \(+V_R/2\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Example 10: 10‑bit Bipolar DAC
10‑bit, bipolar, \(V_R = 5\ \mathrm{V}\).

Inputs:

04FH = \(79_{10}\).
2A4H = \(676_{10}\).

Use Equation (7):

For 79:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{79}{1024}(5) - \frac{5}{2} \approx -2.114\ \mathrm{V}
\]
For 676:
\[
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{676}{1024}(5) - \frac{5}{2} \approx 0.801\ \mathrm{V}
\]

For zero output:

Solve \(0 = \frac{N}{1024}5 - \frac{5}{2}\) → \(N = 512 = 200\mathrm{H} = 1000000000_2\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
DAC Resolution Design Example
Spec: Find number of bits so that \(\Delta V_{\text{out}} \le 0.04\ \mathrm{V}\) with \(V_R = 10\ \mathrm{V}\).
Use Equation (8):
\[
\Delta V = V_R 2^{-n} \le 0.04
\]
\[
0.04 = 10 \cdot 2^{-n} \Rightarrow 2^{-n} = 0.004
\]
Solve for \(n\):
\[
n = \frac{\log(10) - \log(0.04)}{\log 2} \approx 7.97
\]
So choose \(n = 8\).
Check:
\[
\Delta V_{\text{out}} = 10 \cdot 2^{-8} = 0.03906\ \mathrm{V}
\]

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
DAC Block Diagram and Features



Figure 11: Generic DAC diagram.


Key characteristics:

Digital input: parallel \(n\)-bit word (often TTL/CMOS).
Power supply: typically ±12 to ±18 V (or single supply).
Reference supply: sets scale and resolution.
Analog output: stepped voltage (possibly bipolar).
Offset trim: to set true zero output for zero code.
Data latch: hold output value until new word is latched.
Conversion time: almost instantaneous (just amplifier settling, μs range).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Ladder DAC Internal Structure
Most integrated DACs use an R–2R ladder network.



Figure 12: R-2R resistor ladder DAC.



Each bit controls a switch connecting a ladder node to \(V_R\) or ground.
Network ensures each bit contributes the correct weight \(2^{-k}\).
Output is typically buffered by an op‑amp.

You usually treat this as a black box and use Equation (5) or (6).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Example 12: DAC Controlling a Valve
Valve opening is linear from 0 to 10 V. Controlled by 8‑bit DAC fed by microcomputer.
(a) Reference for full‑open at 10 V?
Full‑scale digital code: \(11111111_2 = 255_{10}\). Maximum DAC fraction: \((2^8 - 1)/2^8 = 0.9961\).
\[
10\ \mathrm{V} = 0.9961 V_R \Rightarrow V_R = \frac{10}{0.9961} \approx 10.039\ \mathrm{V}
\]
(b) Percentage valve opening per 1‑bit change?
Resolution:
\[
\Delta V_{\text{out}} = V_R 2^{-8} \approx 10.039/256 \approx 0.0392\ \mathrm{V}
\]
Percentage of full scale (10 V):
\[
\% = \frac{0.0392}{10} \cdot 100 \approx 0.392\%
\]

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
3.3 Analog‑to‑Digital Converters (ADCs) – Concept
An ADC performs the inverse of the DAC:

Input: analog voltage \(V_{\text{in}}\) (plus a reference \(V_R\)).
Output: digital word \(b_1 b_2 \ldots b_n\).

Unipolar ADC relationship:
\[
b_1 2^{-1} + b_2 2^{-2} + \dots + b_n 2^{-n} \le \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_R}
\tag{9}
\]

Left side = fraction represented by digital code.
Right side = scaled input \(V_{\text{in}}/V_R\).
ADC chooses the largest code that does not exceed \(V_{\text{in}}/V_R\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
ADC Quantization Step and Uncertainty
ADC output can only change in increments of \(2^{-n}\) in the normalized fraction.
So the voltage uncertainty for a given code is:
\[
\Delta V = V_R 2^{-n}
\tag{10}
\]
Same as DAC resolution.
Interpretation:

For a given code, actual \(V_{\text{in}}\) is known only within a bin of width \(\Delta V\).
Lower bins: \(0\) to \(\Delta V\), etc.
Top bin: \(V_R (1 - 2^{-n})\) to just below \(V_R\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: Example 13: ADC Range and Resolution from Temperature Sensor
text:
Example 13: ADC Range and Resolution from Temperature Sensor
Sensor: \(V = 0.02\ \mathrm{V/^\circ C} \cdot T\). Measure 0 to \(100^\circ\mathrm{C}\) with \(0.1^\circ\mathrm{C}\) resolution.

Max voltage at 100°C:

\[
(0.02\ \mathrm{V/^\circ C})(100^\circ\mathrm{C}) = 2.0\ \mathrm{V}
\]
So choose \(V_R = 2.0\ \mathrm{V}\).

Required voltage change for 0.1°C:

\[
\Delta V_{\text{required}} = (0.1^\circ\mathrm{C})(0.02\ \mathrm{V/^\circ C}) = 2\ \mathrm{mV}
\]
We need \(\Delta V = V_R 2^{-n} \le 2\ \mathrm{mV}\).
\[
0.002 = 2 \cdot 2^{-n}
\Rightarrow 2^{-n} = 0.001
\Rightarrow n \approx 9.996 \approx 10
\]
So choose 10 bits.
Actual resolution:
\[
\Delta V = 2 \cdot 2^{-10} \approx 0.00195\ \mathrm{V}
\]
Temperature resolution ≈ \(0.0975^\circ\mathrm{C}\), slightly better than required.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: ADC Code Limits – Min and Max
text:
ADC Code Limits – Min and Max

If output is all zeros:

We know \(0 \le V_{\text{in}} < V_R 2^{-n}\).

If output is all ones:

We know \(V_{\text{in}} \ge V_R(1 - 2^{-n})\).


Using example above:

10‑bit, \(V_R = 2\ \mathrm{V}\):

\(V_L = 0\).
Top code (all ones) step at


\[
V_{\text{step}} = V_R (1 - 2^{-10}) \approx 1.9980\ \mathrm{V}
\]
→ corresponds to about \(99.90^\circ\mathrm{C}\).






Warning


So, in practice, we measure slightly less than full physical range (e.g., 0.1°C to 99.9°C).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Fast ADC Code Computation – Equation (11)
Instead of binary fractions, we can directly compute integer code \(N\):
\[
N = \mathrm{INT}\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_R} 2^n\right)
\tag{11}
\]

\(\mathrm{INT}()\) is truncate, not round: \(\mathrm{INT}(3.99) = 3\).

Then convert \(N\) to binary or hex.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Example 14: 5‑bit ADC for 3.127 V Input
5‑bit ADC, \(V_R = 5\ \mathrm{V}\), \(V_{\text{in}} = 3.127\ \mathrm{V}\).
Normalized value:
\[
\frac{3.127}{5} = 0.6254
\]
Binary fraction method (step by step) yields code \(10100_2\).
Using Equation (11):
\[
N = \mathrm{INT}\left(\frac{3.127}{5} \cdot 2^5\right)
= \mathrm{INT}(20.0128) = 20
\]
20 in hex = 14H = \(10100_2\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: Example 15: 10‑bit ADC, Find Code and Back‑Compute Voltage
text:
Example 15: 10‑bit ADC, Find Code and Back‑Compute Voltage
10‑bit, \(V_R = 2.500\ \mathrm{V}\).
(a) Input 1.45 V → find output code
\[
N = \mathrm{INT}\left(\frac{1.45}{2.5} 2^{10}\right)
= \mathrm{INT}(593.92) = 593
\]
593 in hex = 251H.
(b) Given output 1B4H → find possible input range
1B4H = \(436_{10}\).
Center of bin:
\[
V_{\text{in,mid}} = \frac{436}{1024} \cdot 2.5 \approx 1.06445\ \mathrm{V}
\]
Bin width:
\[
\Delta V = V_R 2^{-n} = 2.5 / 1024 \approx 0.00244\ \mathrm{V}
\]
So valid input for code 1B4H:
\[
1.06445\ \mathrm{V} \le V_{\text{in}} < 1.06689\ \mathrm{V}
\]

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Bipolar ADC: Offset Binary Equation
Bipolar ADC (offset binary mapping) formula:
\[
N = \mathrm{INT}\left[\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_R} + \frac{1}{2}\right) 2^n\right]
\tag{12}
\]

\(V_{\text{in}} = -V_R/2\) → \(N = 0\).
\(V_{\text{in}} = 0\) → \(N \approx 2^{n-1}\).
\(V_{\text{in}} \approx +V_R/2\) → \(N \approx 2^n - 1\) (slightly less due to step size).

Example mapping for 8‑bit, \(V_R = 10\ \mathrm{V}\):

\(-5.000\ \mathrm{V} \Rightarrow 00000000_2\).
\(-0.039\ \mathrm{V} \Rightarrow 01111111_2\).
\(0.000\ \mathrm{V} \Rightarrow 10000000_2\).
\(+0.039\ \mathrm{V} \Rightarrow 10000001_2\).
\(+4.961\ \mathrm{V} \Rightarrow 11111111_2\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: Example: Bipolar 8‑bit ADC with 5 V Reference
text:
Example: Bipolar 8‑bit ADC with 5 V Reference
8‑bit, bipolar, \(V_R = 5.00\ \mathrm{V}\).
(a) Input \(V_{\text{in}} = -0.85\ \mathrm{V}\).
\[
N = \mathrm{INT}\left[\left(\frac{-0.85}{5} + 0.5\right) 256\right]
= \mathrm{INT}(84.48) = 84
\]
84 = 54H = \(01010100_2\).
(b) Input \(V_{\text{in}} = +1.5\ \mathrm{V}\).
\[
N = \mathrm{INT}\left[\left(\frac{1.5}{5} + 0.5\right) 256\right]
= \mathrm{INT}(204.8) = 204
\]
204 = CCH = \(11001100_2\).
(c) For output 72H (\(114_{10}\)), solve for \(V_{\text{in}}\):
\[
V_{\text{in}} = \frac{N}{2^n}V_R - \frac{V_R}{2}
= \frac{114}{256} 5.0 - 2.5 \approx -0.2734\ \mathrm{V}
\]
Valid actual input: \([-0.2734,\ -0.2539)\ \mathrm{V}\).

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
ADC Block Diagram and Timing



Figure 13: Generic ADC diagram.


Key points:

Analog input – should remain constant during conversion.
Power supplies – analog ± rails + digital Vcc.
Reference voltage – sets range/resolution.
Digital outputs – \(n\) parallel bits, often tri‑state.
Control lines:

SC (Start Convert).
EOC (End Of Convert).
RD (Read / output enable).


Conversion is not instantaneous → we need to respect conversion time in system design.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Typical ADC Data Acquisition Timing



Figure 14: Data-acquisition timing with ADC.


Sequence:

MCU drives SC low → ADC begins conversion.
ADC asserts EOC high during conversion.
When conversion complete → EOC falls.
MCU waits for EOC, then asserts RD low → digital output appears on lines.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
ADC Architectures: Successive Approximation
Parallel‑feedback (Successive Approximation) ADC:



Figure 15: Successive-approximation ADC using DAC feedback.


Components:

Internal DAC.
Comparator comparing input \(V_x\) with DAC output \(V_F\).
Successive approximation register (SAR) logic to set/test bits.

Operation:

Start with all bits 0.
Set MSB = 1, compute \(V_F\).
If \(V_x \ge V_F\), keep bit = 1; else reset to 0.
Move to next bit; repeat until LSB.

Conversion time ≈ constant: ~1–5 μs/bit.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
section: Example 17: 4‑bit SAR ADC for 3.217 V, Vref = 5 V

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
text:
Dual‑Slope Ramp ADC
Used in digital multimeters and precise instruments.



Figure 16: Dual-slope ADC using integrator and comparator.


Steps:

Integrate input \(V_x\) for fixed time \(T_1\). Output at integrator:

\[
V_1 = \frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{T_1} V_x\,dt = \frac{T_1}{RC} V_x
\tag{14}
\]

Switch input to \(-V_R\); integrator ramps back down.

\[
V_2 = V_1 - \frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{t_x} V_R\,dt = \frac{T_1}{RC} V_x - \frac{t_x}{RC} V_R
\tag{16}
\]

When \(V_2 = 0\), comparator stops counter at time \(t_x\):

\[
0 = \frac{T_1}{RC} V_x - \frac{t_x}{RC} V_R
\Rightarrow V_x = \frac{t_x}{T_1} V_R
\tag{17}
\]
Key: Output code ∝ \(t_x\), independent of R and C tolerances.

title: Converters: Comparators, DACs, and ADCs
